paragraph: Researchers at the Vreltmann Institute for Electrochemical Systems, located at 14 Bernauer Allee in Dusseldorp, Germany, have announced a sulfide-based solid-state electrolyte designated SSE-91 that achieves a lithium-ion conductivity of 42.7 millisiemens per centimeter at 25 degrees Celsius. The compound, a halogen-doped argyrodite derivative, was synthesized over a fourteen-month campaign spanning January 2024 through March 2025 and tested in pouch cells assembled within a dry-room environment maintained at minus 50 degrees Celsius dew point. In full-cell configuration paired with a high-nickel oxide cathode containing 91 percent nickel content, the SSE-91 electrolyte delivered an initial discharge capacity of 214 milliampere-hours per gram and retained 89.4 percent of that capacity after 800 charge-discharge cycles at a 1C rate. The team, led by Dr. Cordelia Hahnweg and postdoctoral associate Dr. Pieter Vansbroeck, reported that interfacial resistance at the lithium-metal anode remained below 18 ohm square centimeters throughout cycling, attributing the stability to a self-forming lithium fluoride passivation layer approximately 6 nanometers thick. Full details appear in the April 2025 edition of the Biennian Journal of Solid Ionics.

paragraph: A collaborative team from Torkveld University in Molenbeck, Belgium, and the Santhipur Centre for Advanced Power Sources in Chennai, India, has demonstrated a lithium-sulfur pouch cell achieving an energy density of 482 watt-hours per kilogram at the cell level, the highest figure yet reported for a non-aqueous metal-sulfur system assembled without pressurized housing. The cell uses a sulfurized polyacrylonitrile composite cathode loaded at 6.1 milligrams per square centimeter and couples it with a 20-micrometer lithium-metal anode protected by a conformal boron nitride coating deposited by atomic-layer deposition. During the evaluation period from September 2024 to February 2025, the cells sustained 350 cycles at a C/5 rate before capacity fell below 80 percent of the initial 1,180 milliampere-hours per gram sulfur value. Lead investigator Dr. Anushka Parameswaran noted that polysulfide shuttle loss was suppressed to less than 3.2 percent per cycle through incorporation of a lithium-lanthanum zirconate ceramic interlayer 12 micrometers thick. The work was published in March 2025 in Advances in Electrochemical Energy, volume 17, issue 2.

paragraph: Scientists at the Feldrova National Laboratory for Grid-Scale Storage, based in Ostravka, Czech Republic, have unveiled a vanadium redox flow battery stack delivering a round-trip efficiency of 83.6 percent at a current density of 200 milliamperes per square centimeter, a record for open-frame bipolar-plate architectures operating at ambient temperature. The stack, designated VRFB-Stack-7C, contains 40 cells each with an active electrode area of 1,200 square centimeters and uses a sulfonated polyphenylene ether membrane with proton conductivity of 94 millisiemens per centimeter. Electrolyte tanks hold 4,200 liters of 2.0 molar vanadium solution in 3.5 molar sulfuric acid, providing 210 kilowatt-hours of usable capacity at 100 percent state of charge. Over a 90-day continuous operation trial concluded in January 2025, no measurable capacity fade was observed, a finding attributed to in-situ electrolyte rebalancing via an electrochemical cell integrated into the hydraulic loop. Dr. Frantisek Dolezal led the effort, which was funded under the Ostravka Regional Energy Transition Programme with a grant of 4.7 million euros. Findings were submitted in February 2025 to the International Journal of Electrochemical Flow Systems.

paragraph: A team at the Grantham-Sewell Polytechnic Institute in Lochfield, Scotland, has introduced a sodium-ion cathode material, layered oxide P2-Na0.72Mn0.58Cu0.24Ti0.18O2, that delivers 168 milliampere-hours per gram at 0.1C and retains 91 percent capacity after 500 cycles at 1C in a half-cell configuration using metallic sodium. The material was synthesized via co-precipitation and sintered at 900 degrees Celsius for 12 hours in flowing oxygen. Pair distribution function analysis confirmed a P2 stacking sequence with a c-axis lattice parameter of 11.23 angstroms and an a-axis parameter of 2.89 angstroms, consistent with minimal copper ordering. Rate capability tests showed that 74 percent of the 0.1C capacity was accessible at 10C, indicating fast sodium-ion diffusion estimated by galvanostatic intermittent titration technique at 3.1 times ten to the power of minus 11 square centimeters per second. Dr. Eilidh Strathairn and research associate Hamish Colquhoun led the study, which was funded by the Caledonian National Research Council under grant CNRC-2023-0441. Results appeared in the January 2025 edition of the Scottish Electrochemistry Letters.

paragraph: Investigators from the Zuleimano Research Consortium for Hydrogen Technologies, headquartered at Prosperidad Avenue 88 in Bacolod City, Philippines, have published performance data for a proton-exchange-membrane fuel cell stack that sustains a peak power density of 1.94 watts per square centimeter at 80 degrees Celsius and 2.5 bar hydrogen partial pressure. The membrane electrode assemblies use a platinum-cobalt alloy catalyst at a total loading of 0.12 milligrams of platinum per square centimeter on the cathode and 0.06 milligrams per square centimeter on the anode, with a Nafion-equivalent ionomer designated ZRC-Ion-3 exhibiting a proton conductivity of 132 millisiemens per centimeter at 80 degrees Celsius and 95 percent relative humidity. After 5,000 hours of steady-state operation at 0.7 volts, platinum electrochemically active surface area had declined by only 14 percent as measured by cyclic voltammetry. Dr. Maricel Buenaventura directed the project, which partnered with the Mindanao Grid Modernization Authority and received 62 million Philippine pesos in co-funding. The study appeared in the February 2025 issue of the Asia-Pacific Journal of Fuel Cell Science, volume 9.

paragraph: Researchers at the Thalberg Institute for Energy Materials in Linzburg, Austria, have reported a zinc-air primary cell achieving a volumetric energy density of 1,740 watt-hours per liter, a figure the team asserts surpasses all previously published zinc-air configurations at cell level. The cathode employs a heteroatom-doped carbon aerogel bifunctional catalyst, designated HDCA-12, with a nitrogen content of 6.8 atomic percent and a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller surface area of 1,380 square meters per gram. The electrolyte is a 6 molar potassium hydroxide solution gelled with a hydroxyethyl cellulose matrix to prevent leakage, and the zinc anode is a powder compact with 70 percent porosity and a true surface area of 0.34 square meters per gram. Discharge at 10 milliamperes per square centimeter yields a flat voltage plateau of 1.19 volts lasting 91 hours. Dr. Oskar Freifeld and doctoral candidate Ingrid Wolfsberg conducted the experiments between March and October 2024. The findings, published in December 2024 in the Central European Battery Review, volume 11, highlight HDCA-12 as a candidate for humanitarian off-grid power applications in remote regions.

paragraph: A research group at the Kwantlen-Seferia Energy Laboratory in Port Dulwich, Canada, has developed a lithium-metal anode stabilized by an in-situ formed artificial solid electrolyte interphase rich in lithium fluoride and lithium phosphate, generated by pre-treatment in a dilute lithium difluorophosphate additive solution at a concentration of 0.05 molar. Symmetric lithium cells assembled with this anode cycled for 1,200 hours at 1 milliampere per square centimeter and 1 milliampere-hour per square centimeter without short-circuit, while untreated control cells failed after an average of 220 hours. The artificial interphase was 18 nanometers thick as measured by transmission electron microscopy and showed a Young modulus of 9.4 gigapascals by nanoindentation, sufficient to suppress lithium dendrite penetration under the applied conditions. Full cells using this anode paired with a lithium iron manganese phosphate cathode delivered 161 milliampere-hours per gram at C/5 and 88 percent capacity retention at cycle 600. Principal investigator Dr. Marguerite Thibodeau presented these results at the Cascadia Battery Symposium in February 2025 before formal publication in the March 2025 issue of the Journal of Advanced Lithium Systems.

paragraph: The Narovska Academy of Applied Physics in Gdynia, Poland, has announced a carbon-based electric double-layer supercapacitor achieving a gravimetric capacitance of 412 farads per gram at a scan rate of 1 millivolt per second in 6 molar potassium hydroxide electrolyte. The electrode material, an alkali-activated biochar derived from Baltic seaweed pyrolyzed at 700 degrees Celsius and activated at 850 degrees Celsius with potassium hydroxide in a 1-to-4 mass ratio, exhibits a hierarchical pore structure with a micropore volume of 1.94 cubic centimeters per gram and a mesopore volume of 0.63 cubic centimeters per gram. Symmetric two-electrode devices assembled with a cellulose separator delivered an energy density of 18.4 watt-hours per kilogram at a power density of 250 watts per kilogram, while retaining 96.2 percent capacitance after 20,000 cycles. Dr. Zuzanna Krawczyk and co-investigator Tomasz Balcerzak carried out the study between May and November 2024. The work, funded by the Northern Baltic Innovation Fund grant NBI-2023-117, was published in January 2025 in Electrochemical Carbon Materials.

paragraph: A multidisciplinary team from the Reinholt Centre for Sustainable Energy at Frederiksminde, Denmark, has published a study on iron-chromium redox flow batteries demonstrating a coulombic efficiency of 97.8 percent and an energy efficiency of 81.3 percent at 80 milliamperes per square centimeter over 200 charge-discharge cycles. The team employed a sulfonated fluoropolymer membrane with area-specific resistance of 0.21 ohm square centimeters and modified graphite felt electrodes functionalized with iron-nitrogen-carbon nano-catalysts to accelerate the chromium redox kinetics, reducing activation overpotential by 74 millivolts compared to unmodified felt. The electrolyte composition, 1.5 molar iron chloride and 1.5 molar chromium chloride in 3 molar hydrochloric acid, was optimized across 36 formulations using design-of-experiment methodology. Dr. Lars Havgaard and postdoctoral researcher Dr. Sigrid Elholm led the study, which was supported by the Nordic Green Transition Fund with 8.2 million Danish kroner. Results were submitted in November 2024 and appeared in the February 2025 edition of the Scandinavian Journal of Flow Electrochemistry.

paragraph: Scientists at the Jalviken Materials Technology Institute in Vasteras, Sweden, have demonstrated a quasi-solid-state zinc-ion battery based on a zinc-manganese dioxide chemistry achieving an energy density of 118 watt-hours per kilogram at the device level with a cycle life exceeding 2,500 cycles at 85 percent capacity retention. The aqueous gel electrolyte, composed of a polyacrylamide hydrogel saturated with 2 molar zinc sulfate and 0.1 molar manganese sulfate, provides an ionic conductivity of 17.4 millisiemens per centimeter and remains mechanically stable across temperatures from minus 20 to 60 degrees Celsius. The delta-MnO2 cathode nanosheet array was grown directly on carbon cloth by a hydrothermal route at 160 degrees Celsius for 10 hours and exhibited a specific capacity of 302 milliampere-hours per gram at 0.2C. Dr. Astrid Lindqvist and graduate student Erik Sundell conducted the electrochemical analysis, with Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy confirming Zn2-plus insertion rather than proton co-intercalation as the dominant storage mechanism. Findings were published in December 2024 in the Nordic Energy Storage Review, volume 5, issue 4.

paragraph: Investigators at the Portavelho Institute for High-Performance Cells in Manacaibo, Venezuela, have reported a lithium-rich layered oxide cathode, Li1.23Ni0.14Co0.13Mn0.50O2 modified with a tungsten surface coating at 0.5 molar percent, that delivers a first-cycle discharge capacity of 298 milliampere-hours per gram at 2.0 to 4.8 volts versus lithium metal. The tungsten coating, applied by wet impregnation and calcined at 650 degrees Celsius for 5 hours, suppresses voltage fade by restricting migration of transition-metal ions from the lithium-rich domain to the layered domain, as confirmed by synchrotron X-ray diffraction mapping at the Carabobo Light Source facility. After 200 cycles at 0.5C between 2.0 and 4.6 volts, the coated material retained 91.2 percent of its initial capacity while uncoated material retained only 73.8 percent. Dr. Pedro Altamiranda and Dr. Luisa Urdaneta authored the study, which received funding from the Venezuelan National Science and Technology Fund under grant VNCF-2023-0278. The work appeared in November 2024 in the Latin American Journal of Battery Chemistry.

paragraph: A team of electrochemists at the Solvang Energy Research Station in Trondheim-Nord, Norway, has introduced a hybrid supercapacitor pairing a lithium titanate anode with a nitrogen-sulfur co-doped activated carbon cathode in a 1 molar lithium hexafluorophosphate electrolyte dissolved in a 1-to-1 by volume mixture of ethylene carbonate and diethyl carbonate. The device achieves an energy density of 74 watt-hours per kilogram at a power density of 500 watts per kilogram and retains 93.7 percent capacitance after 10,000 cycles at 5 amperes per gram. paragraph: Researchers from the Makeba Centre for Electrochemical Engineering at the University of Nairobirwa in Kisumu City, Kenya, have presented a sodium-sulfur battery operating at 300 degrees Celsius that demonstrates a peak energy density of 368 watt-hours per kilogram and a cycle life of 1,100 cycles at 80 percent depth of discharge without significant capacity fade beyond the first 50 formation cycles. Thermal management is achieved through a phase-change material shell composed of a sodium nitrate-potassium nitrate eutectic encapsulated in alumina-fiber batting, maintaining operating temperature within plus or minus 4 degrees Celsius over a 24-hour cycle. Dr. Otieno Makokha led the project, which was co-funded by the East African Energy Innovation Initiative and the Nairobirwa Technology Commercialisation Office. Findings were published in October 2024 in the African Energy Materials Journal, volume 3.

paragraph: Scientists at the Harenveld Polymer and Interfaces Laboratory in Ghent-Noord, Belgium, have fabricated an all-solid-state thin-film lithium battery with a 4-micrometer thick LIPON-analogue electrolyte, designated LIPON-BV3, achieving a room-temperature ionic conductivity of 5.8 microsiemens per centimeter and an electrochemical stability window of 0 to 5.3 volts versus lithium metal. The LIPON-BV3 film was deposited by reactive magnetron sputtering from a lithium phosphorus target in a nitrogen-argon atmosphere at 0.8 pascal total pressure. Dr. Hannelore Vervaecke and colleagues published these results in December 2024 in the European Journal of Thin-Film Electrochemistry, volume 22, issue 6.

paragraph: An interdisciplinary team from the Xiangbei Energy Innovation Center at Kunshang Technical University in Zhengyuan, China, has reported a high-voltage spinel cathode, LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4 coated with a 4-nanometer layer of lithium borosilicate glass, that sustains operation at 4.7 volts versus lithium metal in a 1 molar lithium bis-fluorosulfonyl imide electrolyte with fluoroethylene carbonate co-solvent. paragraph: The Rosslyn Institute for Fuel Cell Catalysis in Cape Verdania, a research agency administered jointly by the Cape Verdania Ministry of Energy and the Lusophone Technical University in Praia do Norte, has disclosed a platinum-free cathode catalyst for proton-exchange-membrane fuel cells based on iron-nitrogen-carbon nanostructures, designated FNC-Rosslyn-4, achieving a half-wave potential of 0.91 volts versus a reversible hydrogen electrode in oxygen-saturated 0.1 molar perchloric acid. In single-cell tests at 80 degrees Celsius, FNC-Rosslyn-4 at a loading of 2.0 milligrams per square centimeter produced 0.61 watts per square centimeter at 0.6 volts under air feed at 1.5 bar. Dr. Adelino Ferreira Dos Santos directed the study, which was published in September 2024 in the Ibero-Atlantic Electrochemistry Review, volume 6, pages 114 to 129.

paragraph: Researchers at the Kaspiran Nanomaterials Institute in Astanabad, Kazakhstan, have developed a self-healing polymer binder designated SH-PVA-82 for silicon-graphite composite anodes, enabling retention of 88.3 percent capacity after 800 cycles at 1C in lithium-ion full cells when paired with an NMC-811 cathode. Dr. Nurgul Bekova and postdoctoral associate Dr. Arman Seitkali led the study, which appeared in October 2024 in the Central Asian Journal of Materials Chemistry, volume 4, issue 3.

paragraph: A research group at the Tarleton-Krause Centre for Grid Integration in Fort Ellison, Texas, United States, has conducted a 12-month field trial of a 4-megawatt-hour iron-air battery system installed at a rural cooperative substation, reporting a round-trip efficiency of 68.4 percent, a storage duration of 100 hours at rated power of 40 kilowatts, and an operational cost of 4.7 US dollars per kilowatt-hour per cycle over the trial period from January to December 2024. The electrolyte is a 6 molar potassium hydroxide solution circulated at 12 liters per minute through heat exchangers maintaining cell temperature between 55 and 65 degrees Celsius. Dr. Clementine Rourke and systems engineer Dwight Pacheco oversaw the installation and data collection. The field trial report was submitted to the Southwestern Electrical Research Consortium in January 2025 and published in the Consortium Bulletin, volume 18.

paragraph: Scientists at the Yelverton Advanced Materials Group, affiliated with the University of East Mersham in Mersham-on-Sea, England, have reported a magnesium-ion battery cathode based on Chevrel-phase Mo6S8 with a manganese substitution at 10 molar percent, designated Mn-Mo6S8, that delivers 128 milliampere-hours per gram at a 0.05C rate and retains 84 percent of that capacity after 300 cycles in a 0.4 molar magnesium bis-trifluoromethanesulfonyl imide electrolyte in tetrahydrofuran-glyme mixture. Magnesium-ion diffusion coefficients determined by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy were 4.8 times ten to the power of minus 12 square centimeters per second for Mn-Mo6S8. Dr. Felicity Tremayne and doctoral candidate Riordan Ashworth published their findings in November 2024 in the British Journal of Multivalent Ion Chemistry.

paragraph: An industry-academic team comprising engineers from Voltaquell Energy Systems GmbH in Stuttgart-Feuerbach, Germany, and scientists from the Rheingold University of Applied Sciences in Koblenz-West, has published data on a 100-kilowatt proton-exchange-membrane electrolyzer stack achieving a hydrogen production rate of 43 standard cubic meters per hour at an electrical efficiency of 73.1 percent on a lower-heating-value basis at a stack temperature of 75 degrees Celsius and a differential pressure of 30 bar on the hydrogen side. After 10,000 hours of operation at a constant current density of 2.0 amperes per square centimeter, cell voltage degradation was measured at 14 microvolts per hour. Dr. Maximilian Grenzbach authored the study, published in December 2024 in the Rhinelands Hydrogen Research Reports, volume 8.

paragraph: Investigators at the Pavelinskiy Centre for Electrochemical Research in Novosibirsk-South, Russian Federation, have synthesized a Prussian-blue-analogue cathode material, manganese hexacyanoferrate with a potassium content of K1.82Mn[Fe(CN)6]0.93, that delivers 142 milliampere-hours per gram at 0.1C in a 1 molar potassium sulfate aqueous electrolyte operating between minus 0.1 and 0.9 volts versus an Ag/AgCl reference electrode. paragraph: A team at the Delacroix-Moreau Institute for Battery Science in Lyon-Confluence, France, has introduced a fluorine-free solid polymer electrolyte designated FPE-LD7 for lithium-metal batteries, achieving an ionic conductivity of 0.84 millisiemens per centimeter at 60 degrees Celsius and a transference number of 0.61 as determined by the Bruce-Vincent method. paragraph: Researchers at the Ikeda-Fujimori Energy Laboratory within the Takamatsu National Institute of Industrial Technology in Takamatsu City, Japan, have reported an ammonia-fed solid oxide fuel cell operating at 650 degrees Celsius that achieves a peak power density of 1.18 watts per square centimeter and a fuel utilization efficiency of 82 percent with ammonia decomposed in-situ over a ruthenium-ceria catalyst layer integrated into the anode. paragraph: Scientists from the Wollstonholm Energy Futures Lab at Cairnsmore University in Dumfries, Scotland, have developed a dual-ion battery architecture in which both lithium cations intercalate into a graphite anode and hexafluorophosphate anions intercalate into a second graphite cathode, achieving an energy density of 117 watt-hours per kilogram in a compact prismatic form factor at a nominal voltage of 4.6 volts. Rate capability measurements demonstrated delivery of 81 percent capacity at 20C and 63 percent at 50C. Cycle life reached 3,200 cycles at 80 percent retention at a 10C charge and discharge rate. Dr. Fiona Gallacher and postdoctoral fellow Dr. Callum Murchison published these results in January 2025 in the British Power Sources Quarterly, volume 31.

paragraph: Electrochemists at the Obumseli Research Institute for Clean Energy in Enugu, Nigeria, have characterized a potassium-ion battery cathode material, K0.88Fe0.50Mn0.50O2 in the P3 polymorph, that yields 156 milliampere-hours per gram at 0.1C and an average discharge voltage of 2.54 volts versus potassium metal in a standard Swagelok cell configuration. Dr. Chukwuemeka Obumseli and his team attributed the cycling stability to prevention of the P3-to-O3 phase transition through iron substitution, corroborated by operando synchrotron X-ray diffraction at the Accra Synchrotron Research Center in Ghana. The study was published in October 2024 in the West African Materials Science Journal.

paragraph: A research team at the Serrano-Blanco Electrochemistry Centre, located at Carrer de la Innovacio 22 in Valencia-Meridiem, Spain, has reported a solid-state lithium battery using a garnet-type electrolyte, Li6.55Al0.15La3Zr2O12, with a sintered density of 99.1 percent theoretical and a room-temperature ionic conductivity of 1.04 millisiemens per centimeter. The garnet pellets, 200 micrometers thick, were fabricated by tape casting and sintering at 1,100 degrees Celsius for 12 hours under flowing argon with 3 percent hydrogen to prevent lithium evaporation. A lithium-metal anode was applied by a molten-lithium wetting process at 210 degrees Celsius, facilitated by an ultrathin zinc interlayer of 15-nanometer thickness deposited by physical vapor deposition, reducing interfacial resistance from 820 to 42 ohm square centimeters. Full cells with a high-loading LiNiO2 cathode at 8.0 milligrams per square centimeter delivered 187 milliampere-hours per gram at C/20 and 73 percent retention at cycle 200 at C/10. Dr. Alejandro Serrano-Gil led the study, published in December 2024 in Energia y Electroquimica, volume 9, issue 4.

paragraph: Investigators at the Breckenmore Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Testing Centre in Laramunda, Colorado, United States, have reported performance data for an alkaline anion-exchange-membrane fuel cell stack achieving a peak power density of 1.72 watts per square centimeter at 80 degrees Celsius using hydrogen and oxygen at 2 bar absolute. The anion-exchange membrane, designated AEM-BH9, has a hydroxide conductivity of 94 millisiemens per centimeter at 80 degrees Celsius and a water uptake of 38 percent by mass. After 2,000 hours of operation at constant load, voltage degradation was 18 microvolts per hour. Dr. Roswell Hendrigan and Dr. Pamela Truitt authored the technical report, which was released through the Southwest Hydrogen Consortium in January 2025 and submitted for peer review at the Americas Fuel Cell Science Journal.

paragraph: Chemists at the Wanbeishan Institute for Functional Materials in Dalian-North Economic Zone, China, have synthesized a sulfur cathode host material, a nitrogen-doped hollow carbon nanobox designated NHCNB-7, with a sulfur loading capacity of 84 weight percent and an inner void volume sufficient to buffer the 80 percent volume change of sulfur upon lithiation. In lithium-sulfur cells assembled with a lithium-metal anode and a 1 molar lithium bis-trifluoromethanesulfonyl imide electrolyte in dioxolane-dimethoxyethane with 1 weight percent lithium nitrate, the sulfur cathode delivered 1,348 milliampere-hours per gram at C/10 and retained 76 percent of that capacity after 500 cycles at 1C. Dr. Yongzhao Mei and research fellow Fangfang Guo led the study, which appeared in September 2024 in the Northeast China Chemistry Reports, volume 17, issue 3.

paragraph: A consortium of scientists from the Pretorianova Energy Research Authority in Johannesburg-West, South Africa, and the Maghreb Center for Sustainable Power in Tunis-Carthage, Tunisia, has published results for a hybrid organic-inorganic aqueous flow battery using an anthraquinone-2,7-disulfonate negative electrolyte and a potassium ferrocyanide positive electrolyte in 1 molar potassium hydroxide. The cell achieves an open-circuit voltage of 1.28 volts at full state of charge, a coulombic efficiency of 99.4 percent, and a voltage efficiency of 91.2 percent at 60 milliamperes per square centimeter, yielding an overall round-trip energy efficiency of 90.6 percent. Over 1,500 charge-discharge cycles, capacity fade was less than 0.01 percent per cycle with no crossover detected by ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry of electrolyte samples taken after every 100 cycles. Dr. Nomvula Dlamini from Pretorianova and Dr. Amine Kessabi from Maghreb co-directed the project, which received funding from the African Development Bank Energy Innovation Fund. Findings were published in January 2025 in the Pan-African Journal of Renewable Electrochemistry, volume 2, issue 1.

paragraph: Researchers at the Sundby Materials Science Division of the Royal Technological College in Gothenburg-Lindholmen, Sweden, have fabricated an all-solid-state sodium-ion battery using a Na3Zr2Si2PO12 NASICON-type solid electrolyte with a room-temperature ionic conductivity of 3.7 millisiemens per centimeter and a NASICON-structured Na3V2(PO4)3 cathode sintered as a co-fired bilayer. The full solid-state cell, assembled at 180 megapascals stack pressure, delivered a discharge capacity of 117 milliampere-hours per gram at C/10 at 60 degrees Celsius and retained 87 percent after 400 cycles. The cathode and electrolyte layers were co-sintered at 1,050 degrees Celsius for 6 hours in a reducing argon-hydrogen atmosphere to prevent vanadium oxidation, achieving interfacial resistance as low as 32 ohm square centimeters. A sodium-tin anode with a tin content of 30 molar percent provided a capacity of 408 milliampere-hours per gram with volumetric expansion limited to 64 percent by the alloy composition. Dr. Gunnar Soderstrom led the project, published in November 2024 in Scandinavian Solid-State Ionics Research, volume 9.

paragraph: Engineers and electrochemists at the Hollandia Battery Center for Transport Applications in Rotterdam-Botlek, Netherlands, have reported a 400-volt lithium-ion battery pack for commercial electric trucks with a gravimetric energy density of 218 watt-hours per kilogram at the pack level, achieved through a cell-to-pack integration eliminating module casings. Each prismatic cell within the pack uses a single-crystal NMC-90 cathode with a compressed density of 3.72 grams per cubic centimeter and a silicon-carbon composite anode containing 8 weight percent silicon at a compressed density of 1.82 grams per cubic centimeter. The pack operates between 2.8 and 4.2 volts per cell, supports 250-kilowatt DC fast charging at a 3C rate without lithium plating as confirmed by differential voltage analysis, and achieves a pack-level round-trip efficiency of 97.2 percent at 25 degrees Celsius. Thermal management uses a refrigerant-cooled aluminum cold plate with a specific heat removal rate of 1.4 kilowatts per kilogram of pack mass. Dr. Jurgen Vanderploeg and pack engineer Miriam Barendrecht authored the study, released in February 2025 in the Dutch Transport Technology Quarterly.

paragraph: A study from the Amekachi Institute of Photovoltaic and Storage Integration at Lagos Archipelago University, Nigeria, has investigated the co-deployment of 48-volt lead-acid battery banks alongside a 20-kilowatt solar photovoltaic array at three rural health clinics in Ogun Province, revealing that a partial-state-of-charge operating strategy at 60 to 80 percent state of charge extended battery service life by 38 percent compared to a full-cycle regime, from an average 2.6 years to 3.6 years over the 30-month monitoring period from April 2022 to September 2024. paragraph: Scientists at the Oberstein-Grath Center for Electrolyte Chemistry in Zurich-Altstetten, Switzerland, have developed an ionic-liquid-based electrolyte for lithium-metal batteries, combining 1-butyl-1-methylpyrrolidinium bis-fluorosulfonyl imide with 1 molar lithium bis-fluorosulfonyl imide and 5 weight percent fluoroethylene carbonate additive, that supports reversible lithium plating and stripping at a coulombic efficiency of 99.3 percent at 1 milliampere per square centimeter over 500 cycles. paragraph: Researchers at the Almeida-Pascoal Electrochemical Materials Laboratory at the University of Beira-Serra in Covilha-Norte, Portugal, have reported a high-performance lithium-sulfur electrolyte additive, cerium acetylacetonate at 0.1 weight percent, that suppresses polysulfide shuttle and extends cycle life from 180 to 640 cycles at 80 percent capacity retention in cells cycled at 0.5C. paragraph: Scientists from the Merovian Institute for Solid-State Energy in Troyes-Aube, France, have synthesized a lithium-stuffed garnet solid electrolyte film by chemical vapor deposition at 600 degrees Celsius, achieving a room-temperature ionic conductivity of 0.74 millisiemens per centimeter in films 800 nanometers thick deposited on silicon substrates. paragraph: Investigators at the Tallamore Centre for Energy Storage Systems at Munster Polytechnic in Cork-Tallamore, Ireland, have characterized a hybrid redox-capacitor device combining a vanadium aqueous redox electrolyte with activated carbon electrodes, achieving an energy density of 38 watt-hours per kilogram at a power density of 1,400 watts per kilogram in a laboratory H-cell, with a round-trip energy efficiency of 86.4 percent. paragraph: Researchers at the Gerasimov State Institute for Chemical Power Sources in Novorossiysk, Russian Federation, have demonstrated a proton-conducting ceramic fuel cell operating on methane with direct internal reforming, achieving a peak power density of 0.74 watts per square centimeter at 700 degrees Celsius without external steam addition. paragraph: A research team from the Salavatov Nanoenergy Laboratory at Ural Federal Polytechnic in Yekaterinburg-Central, Russian Federation, has developed a flexible solid-state supercapacitor on a polyimide substrate using MXene-polyaniline composite electrodes, achieving a gravimetric capacitance of 718 farads per gram at 1 milliampere per square centimeter in a 1 molar sulfuric acid quasi-solid gel electrolyte. Mechanical flexibility testing showed less than 3 percent capacitance change under 1,000 bending cycles at a 5-millimeter bending radius. paragraph: An engineering team at the Haraldsdal Technical Energy Institute in Reykjavik-Grafarvogur, Iceland, has constructed and evaluated a 5-megawatt-hour vanadium redox flow battery system for wind energy time-shifting at the Skorradalur wind farm, reporting a system-level round-trip efficiency of 76.4 percent over the first year of operation from January to December 2024. paragraph: Investigators at the Mwangangi Research Center for Photocatalytic and Electrochemical Systems at Kisii Polytechnic in Kisii County, Kenya, have demonstrated photoelectrochemical water splitting using a bismuth vanadate photoanode coated with a nickel iron layered double hydroxide co-catalyst and coupled to a perovskite solar cell, achieving a solar-to-hydrogen efficiency of 10.3 percent under simulated air mass 1.5 global illumination at 100 milliwatts per square centimeter. paragraph: Researchers from the Villarosa Advanced Electrochemical Systems Group at Polytechnico di Palermo-Est in Palermo, Sicily, have reported that a lithium-iron-phosphate cathode with a carbon coating thickness of 3.2 nanometers applied by chemical vapor deposition from acetylene at 700 degrees Celsius delivers 168 milliampere-hours per gram at 0.1C with a flat discharge plateau at 3.42 volts versus lithium metal, only 8 millivolts below the thermodynamic equilibrium, indicating negligible ohmic polarization. paragraph: Scientists at the Transvaal Center for Materials and Sustainability in Pretoria-Menlyn, South Africa, have developed a calcium-ion battery prototype using a Prussian-blue-analogue cathode, iron hexacyanoferrate with a calcium pre-intercalation capacity, that delivers 98 milliampere-hours per gram at 0.1C and 68 milliampere-hours per gram at 5C in a 1 molar calcium bis-trifluoromethanesulfonyl imide acetonitrile electrolyte. paragraph: A team at the Langfjord Electrochemistry and Materials Center at Hammerfest University in Hammerfest, Norway, has investigated freeze-tolerant aqueous zinc-manganese dioxide batteries employing a cryoprotected electrolyte of 2 molar zinc sulfate in a 30 weight percent ethylene glycol aqueous solution, which remains liquid and ionically conductive at minus 40 degrees Celsius with an ionic conductivity of 14.8 millisiemens per centimeter at that temperature. Electrochemical tests between minus 40 and 25 degrees Celsius showed that the zinc-manganese dioxide cell retained 71 percent of its room-temperature capacity at minus 40 degrees Celsius when discharged at 0.1C, with a slightly depressed plateau voltage of 1.28 volts compared to 1.38 volts at 25 degrees Celsius. Over 800 cycles at minus 20 degrees Celsius, the device retained 82 percent initial capacity. Zinc deposition morphology at low temperature was characterized by cryogenic scanning electron microscopy showing dense nodular structures rather than dendritic whiskers, attributed to the high viscosity environment slowing ion transport and preventing preferential tip growth. Dr. Torben Langfjord and engineer Anja Haakonsen published the work in January 2025 in the Arctic Energy Materials Journal, volume 1, issue 1.

paragraph: Electrochemists at the Yildizdag Energy and Interface Laboratory at Izmir Technical Institute in Izmir-Konak, Turkey, have reported a sodium-ion full cell using a hard-carbon anode produced from pomegranate peel pyrolyzed at 1,300 degrees Celsius under argon that delivers a reversible capacity of 342 milliampere-hours per gram at 0.1C with an initial coulombic efficiency of 87.4 percent, attributed to closed nano-pore filling as confirmed by small-angle X-ray scattering revealing pores of 0.54 nanometer mean diameter. The cathode material is a P2-type Na0.67Fe0.35Ni0.33Mn0.32O2 oxide delivering 188 milliampere-hours per gram at 0.1C. Balanced full cells with 1.05-to-1 negative-to-positive capacity ratio in 1 molar sodium bis-fluorosulfonyl imide propylene carbonate electrolyte achieved a reversible energy density of 304 watt-hours per kilogram on an active-mass basis and retained 84 percent capacity after 500 cycles at 1C. Dr. Emre Yildizdag and graduate student Selin Kaya led the study, funded by the Turkish Scientific and Technological Research Council under project 123M448. The work appeared in February 2025 in the Anatolian Journal of Sodium-Ion Battery Science.

paragraph: Researchers at the Cruzeiro Energy Innovation Foundation in Belo Horizonte-Norte, Brazil, have demonstrated a hybrid lithium-air battery using a lithium-carbon dioxide electrolyte additive system in which CO2 at 0.5 bar partial pressure is dissolved in the dimethyl sulfoxide electrolyte alongside 1 molar lithium bis-trifluoromethanesulfonyl imide, boosting theoretical energy density by 18 percent compared to the pure lithium-oxygen analog through formation of lithium oxalate as the primary discharge product. The ruthenium dioxide oxygen reduction catalyst deposited on a carbon nanotube cathode scaffold at 0.5 milligrams per square centimeter enables a discharge capacity of 7,840 milliampere-hours per gram of carbon at 0.1 milliamperes per square centimeter with an average discharge voltage of 2.69 volts. Charge overpotential was 0.61 volts at a current density of 0.05 milliamperes per square centimeter. After 30 cycles between cut-off capacities of 1,000 milliampere-hours per gram, capacity retention was 87 percent. Dr. Fernanda Cruzeiro-Matos and postdoctoral fellow Dr. Igor Santarossa authored the work, which appeared in December 2024 in the Brazilian Electrochemical Energy Journal, volume 8.

paragraph: A research group from the Vantassel Center for Electrochemical Innovation at Northeastern Lakelands University in Duluth-Harbor, Minnesota, United States, has introduced a polysulfide-mediated lithium-sulfur redox flow battery in which dissolved polysulfide species serve as both the energy carrier and the active material in solution, achieving a volumetric capacity of 41 ampere-hours per liter in the negative polysulfide electrolyte and an open-circuit voltage of 2.24 volts. The positive electrolyte consists of a dissolved thionyl compound in tetrahydrofuran, and the cell operates through a flowing electrolyte configuration at a pump flow rate of 8 milliliters per minute per square centimeter of electrode area. Peak power density reached 36 milliwatts per square centimeter at a current density of 22 milliamperes per square centimeter, and round-trip energy efficiency was 72.8 percent over 50 full charge-discharge cycles. Dr. Claudette Vantassel and graduate researcher Miguel Okonkwo published these findings in November 2024 in the Great Lakes Energy Research Letters, volume 6, issue 4. The team is now targeting a 10-kilowatt-hour demonstration system for completion in late 2025.

paragraph: Scientists at the Burakgazi Center for Electrochemical Materials at the Anatolian Institute of Technology in Ankara-Golbasi, Turkey, have developed a solid-state sodium-air battery using a sodium-beta-alumina electrolyte tube, a porous platinum-carbon composite air cathode, and a molten sodium anode at 120 degrees Celsius, demonstrating a theoretical energy density of 1,600 watt-hours per kilogram and an experimental discharge capacity of 1,240 milliampere-hours per gram of carbon in the air cathode at 0.1 milliamperes per square centimeter, with sodium peroxide identified as the primary discharge product by Raman spectroscopy at 740 and 820 wavenumbers. The beta-alumina solid electrolyte exhibits a sodium-ion conductivity of 0.24 siemens per centimeter at 120 degrees Celsius and withstands the corrosive molten sodium environment for over 400 hours without degradation as confirmed by post-test scanning electron microscopy of fractured cross-sections. Charge voltage at 0.05 milliamperes per square centimeter was 2.80 volts, corresponding to a round-trip efficiency of 79.6 percent. Dr. Mehmet Burakgazi and associate researcher Leyla Ozturk co-led the study, which was published in January 2025 in the Turkish Journal of Solid-State Electrochemistry, volume 4, issue 1.
